lesson.menu_desc: Talk about your life experiences: contrast finished moments in the past (past simple) with experiences that still matter now (present perfect) — ever, already, yet, lately — 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 2 -- My Story So Far -- Past Simple vs Present Perfect (Life Experiences)
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Sandra Hayasaki | Aula 2 -- My Story So Far

listenings.text: Let me tell you a little about my story so far. I have lived in the same city for most of my life, and I have always loved being close to my family. A few years ago, I started learning English, mostly because I wanted to travel more freely. Back then, I only knew a few words, and I felt nervous every time someone spoke to me in English. I have made a lot of mistakes since then, but each one has taught me something. Last year, I finally took my first trip abroad, and it was one of the proudest moments of my life. I ordered food in English, I asked for directions, and people actually understood me. I have not become fluent yet, but I have already done things I never thought I could do. Lately, I have felt more confident, and I have noticed that my biggest achievement is not perfect grammar. It is the courage to keep speaking, even when it is hard.

Hi Sandra, it is Lucas. I just wanted to leave you a quick voice note before our next class. First, congratulations on everything you have accomplished so far. I have noticed real progress, and I am proud of you. Here is a small tip about telling your story in English. When you talk about a finished moment, with a clear time like last year or in two thousand twenty, use the past simple: I traveled, I started, I felt. But when the experience still connects to now, when the exact time does not matter, use the present perfect: I have traveled abroad, I have already tried that, I have not done it yet. Listen for the little words too. Ever asks about your whole life: have you ever lived abroad? Already means sooner than expected, yet means up to now in questions and negatives, and lately means in the recent past. So before our class, think of three experiences you are proud of, and try to describe each one twice: once as a finished moment, and once as a life experience. See you soon.
